summary: Shaw examines how internet social norms protect trolls while punishing their victims, using a Guardian article about an author who tracked down her Goodreads troll as a launching point. He argues that the 'Don't Feed The Trolls' ethos mirrors classic abuser dynamics, giving trolls full license to harass while penalizing any response from victims. He then expands into a broader hierarchy of creative social status, ranking trolls at the top and nerds at the bottom based on what society lets each group get away with. Drawing from his own experience as someone who occupies multiple roles—troll of trolls, artist, author, musician, coder, and nerd—he illustrates how each identity carries different social privileges and penalties. The overall argument is that internet culture has created perverse incentive structures where the most destructive actors enjoy the most protection.
key_points: Internet norms like 'Don't Feed The Trolls' function as an abuser dynamic, giving trolls license to attack while punishing victims who respond
On platforms like Goodreads, authors are forbidden from responding to reviews, making troll reviewers more trusted than the creators themselves
Shaw proposes a creative social hierarchy where trolls sit at the top and nerds at the bottom, based on what behavior society permits each group
Coders occupy a rising but resented position in the hierarchy, drawing hostility from every other creative class despite building the platforms they all use
Being a nerd overrides all other creative identities, coating everything you do with uncoolness regardless of talent or output
money_quotes: The insanity of the internet is not that there are trolls, it's that trolls are given full rights to abuse others and people defend the troll's right to be abusive fucking assholes.
The internet abuser will send out a small pin prick, hoping for a response so they can justify their abuse because the social norms of the internet gives them license to then attack if you respond.
The point of free speech is that everyone gets to talk, not just the first asshole who opens her mouth.
An abuser's entire goal in life is to find a reason to exert their abusive sociopathic desires onto victims while appearing to be in the right.
You'll see nearly every other sector of the creative social class use Twitter, Wordpress, and their iPhones to rant about how coders are destroying the world with their technology.
Being a nerd is at the bottom of the hierarchy because no matter what I do, my nerdiness coats my creative output with the hot sticky air of the uncool.
I tell people I write 'technical books' and they say, 'Oh so not real books.'
key_insight: Internet culture has constructed social norms that grant trolls more protection and social power than any creative class, mirroring the dynamics of real-world abusive relationships where victims are punished for fighting back.
